description.current: E2E patterns: journey design (one goal per journey, precondition→action→assertion), evidence management (step-NN naming, inventory), flaky flow diagnosis (3 runs, capture delta, fix root).
issues: Description is a pure feature list — doesn't say WHY or WHEN. 'Journey design' doesn't sell the concept.
Mentions 'flaky flow diagnosis' but doesn't explain what flakiness is or why it matters to the reader
Lacks hook: 'When should I read this skill?' Answer is buried in the body ('When designing validation journeys...')

issues.description: Entire 'Journey Design Principles' section (lines 24-87) is high-level guidance but uses abstract bad examples: 'Test the login page (too narrow)' and 'Test everything (too broad)' are vague. No concrete project context (e.g., 'a SaaS app', 'mobile banking').
Journey Independence (lines 70-75) states the rule but doesn't explain CONSEQUENCES of violating it. Why is dependency bad? Cascading failures? Test order dependency?

Evidence Inventory section (lines 117-127) shows a bash command for listing files but doesn't explain HOW to generate the file or WHY 'verdict-writer' needs it. Feels disconnected from actual workflow.

Flaky Flow Handling (lines 129-167) recommends 'run 3 times' and 'capture delta' but never shows HOW to compare deltas or WHAT to look for. No concrete example of 'what changed between PASS and FAIL run?'
Quarantine Pattern (lines 150-167) is excellent but only applies to flaky flows. No guidance on regular (non-flaky) failures.

strengths: Journey Design section (lines 26-68) teaches the correct mental model: one goal per journey, clear precondition→action→assertion structure
Priority matrix (lines 80-86) is concrete and aligns with business value (P0 revenue, P1 core, P2 secondary, P3 edge)
Evidence naming convention (lines 105-115) is unambiguous and sortable (step-NN prefix)
Quarantine Pattern (lines 150-167) is a practical response to flaky tests without introducing mocks or skips
Integration with ValidationForge section (lines 192-198) properly positions this skill as strategic, not tactical

bundled_resources.missing_scripts_opportunity: A script to auto-generate evidence-inventory.txt from a journey directory, or a template to help users structure their first journey (including precondition setup and cleanup).

top_fixes: Rewrite description to lead with WHEN: 'Read this skill when designing validation journeys or diagnosing flaky flows. It teaches journey patterns (one goal per journey), evidence naming discipline, and root-cause diagnosis for intermittent failures — the difference between 'retry and hope' vs 'fix and verify.'

In Journey Design, replace abstract bad examples with concrete ones: '❌ Bad: "Validate login page" (no user goal, no PASS criteria). ✓ Good: "User logs in with token auth and can read /api/profile/", PASS: 200 response with email field.' Make it 2-3 real scenarios.
In Journey Independence, add: 'If Journey B depends on Journey A, and A fails, B won't even run. This masks the root cause — you'll see "B failed" instead of "A failed". Keep journeys independent.'

In Flaky Flow Handling, show a concrete DIFF: 'Run 1 (PASS): screenshot shows dashboard with data, console clean. Run 2 (FAIL): screenshot shows loading spinner, network tab shows /api/data taking 4.5s (timeout is 3s). Root cause: API slow, not test flaky. Fix: increase timeout or optimize API.'
Add section 'Non-Flaky Failures' after Quarantine Pattern: 'If a journey fails consistently, don't quarantine it — it's a real bug. Diagnose and fix with error-recovery skill.'

In Evidence Inventory section, show example output: 'e2e-evidence/new-user-signup/evidence-inventory.txt contains: 1200 step-01-navigate.png, 4800 step-02-form-filled.png, 450 step-03-api-response.json, etc. verdict-writer reads this to find all artifacts for the journey report.'

test_prompts.prompt: I'm designing an E2E journey for my React app. Users can sign up, create a post, and see it in the feed. That's three actions. Should I make it one journey or three separate journeys?
test_prompts.expected_output: Explain: one journey (one user goal: 'new user creates a post'). Precondition: no account. Actions: (1) sign up, (2) create post, (3) navigate to feed. Assertion: post visible in feed. It's atomic. If you split it into three journeys, you lose the end-to-end signal and can't catch the 'post created but not in feed' bug.

test_prompts.prompt: My login journey passes most of the time but fails 1 out of 5 runs. Sometimes the dashboard takes forever to load. I've captured 5 runs of evidence. How do I figure out if it's a real bug or just my test being flaky?
test_prompts.expected_output: Walk through the diagnosis: (1) Look at the PASS runs — does the dashboard load in <1s? (2) Look at the FAIL runs — does it take >3s? (3) Check the network tab in evidence — is the /api/dashboard endpoint actually slow on FAIL runs? If yes: real bug (API performance). If no: test issue (timing). Show how to compare screenshots and network timings, not just 'PASS' vs 'FAIL' verdicts.

test_prompts.prompt: I want to organize evidence for 6 journeys in my validation. Some are P0 (checkout), some are P2 (search filters). What directory structure should I use? How do I make sure I capture the right evidence for each?
test_prompts.expected_output: Show directory structure: e2e-evidence/p0-checkout/, e2e-evidence/p2-search-filters/ (or journey-slug-based: e2e-evidence/new-user-checkout/, etc). For each journey: capture step-NN files in order (step-01, step-02, etc). Naming: step-01-navigate-to-checkout.png, step-02-add-item.png, step-03-payment-form.png, step-04-confirmation.png. Emphasize: each step should be a distinct user action, with visual proof. Generate evidence-inventory.txt to track all files.
